topic: oh-my-pi tool inventory vs pi-mono base — gap-closure for Misul Terminal fork

findings.detail: Full regex search across files, directories, and globs using ripgrep via the @oh-my-pi/pi-natives Rust NAPI module. Supports pagination (skip), case-insensitive flag, gitignore control, per-file match caps, context lines before/after, archive member search (materializes to temp file), internal URL search, and line-range selectors. 1598 lines. The pi-natives `grep()` function is a Rust-compiled NAPI binding.
findings.depsOrPortability: Rust (pi-natives grep binding for ripgrep). The pure-JS fallback path (`searchVirtualResources`) in the same file is a self-contained regex engine over in-memory content and is pure-TS/Node. The filesystem grep path is hard-tied to the pi-natives native module.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — pi-mono (the fork base) does NOT ship a search tool in its coding-agent package at all; the agent package only provides the harness. oh-my-pi's search.ts is the primary code-search capability. The native ripgrep binding cannot be ported to pure-TS; the tool itself must be rebuilt calling a system `rg` binary or a JS ripgrep wrapper. However, the virtual-resource search logic (pure-TS JS regex, ~500 lines) is portable as-is.

findings.detail: Glob-based file and directory finder. Uses @oh-my-pi/pi-natives `glob()` (Rust/ignore crate via NAPI) for fast, gitignore-aware, mtime-sorted results with streaming progress updates. Supports multiple paths, timeout, hidden files, and pluggable FindOperations interface for remote delegation (SSH). 636 lines.
findings.depsOrPortability: Rust (pi-natives glob binding wrapping the `ignore` crate). The FindOperations interface is pure-TS and enables a drop-in replacement backend. Portability to Node: use `fast-glob` or `glob` npm package as the backend through FindOperations.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — the tool architecture (FindOperations pluggable interface) was explicitly designed for this. Replace the native glob with a pure-TS backend (`fast-glob`). The tool's schema, result formatting, TUI renderer, and pagination logic are all pure-TS and copy directly.

findings.detail: Structural AST pattern search backed by `ast-grep-core` (Rust) with 40+ tree-sitter language grammars, all compiled into the pi-natives NAPI module. Searches files and directories by AST pattern, returns matches with metavariable bindings. 483 lines.
findings.depsOrPortability: Hard Rust dependency (ast-grep-core + every tree-sitter grammar, all in pi-natives). No TS fallback exists.
findings.recommendation: skip — the capability is deeply tied to Rust compiled grammars. A minimal Pi fork has no equivalent and forking this would require shipping the full pi-natives binary. The semantic value for a minimal harness is moderate; ripgrep regex search covers most practical use cases without AST awareness.

findings.detail: Structural AST rewrite using ast-grep patterns. Uses pi-natives `astEdit()` NAPI binding. Takes an ops array of {pat, out} rewrite rules and applies them across files/directories. Integrates with the file-snapshot store for conflict detection.
findings.depsOrPortability: Hard Rust dependency (same pi-natives binary as ast-grep). No TS fallback.
findings.recommendation: skip — same rationale as ast-grep. Building on top of the base `write` tool suffices for the minimal fork.

findings.detail: 1938-line multi-backend web reader. Pipeline: special site handlers → HTTP fetch → binary dispatch (PDF/DOCX via markit, SQLite via bun:sqlite, archive via Bun.Archive, images with resize) → HTML-to-markdown via a ranked fallback chain: native (pi-natives htmlToMarkdown Rust), trafilatura (subprocess), lynx (subprocess), Parallel API, Jina API. Also handles llms.txt/llms.md discovery, content negotiation, RSS/Atom parsing, line-range URL selectors, and URL-suffix markdown probing. Bun.Archive, bun:sqlite, and Bun.write are used throughout. The pi-natives `htmlToMarkdown` is the highest-priority HTML converter. The 'read' tool delegates URL handling entirely to this module via `executeReadUrl`.
findings.depsOrPortability: Mixed: pi-natives (htmlToMarkdown — Rust NAPI, highest-priority backend), bun:sqlite (SQLite rendering of fetched .db files), Bun.Archive (archive listing), Bun.write (temp files). Pure-TS/Node portions: all HTTP logic, feed parsing (linkedom), JSON/text handling, fallback ordering, llms.txt discovery, markit subprocess, trafilatura/lynx subprocesses, Parallel/Jina API calls. The native htmlToMarkdown can fall through to trafilatura or lynx without code changes.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — the bulk of fetch.ts is pure-TS HTTP orchestration that requires no native deps. Drop the pi-natives htmlToMarkdown import and rely on trafilatura/lynx/Jina fallbacks, which already exist in the same file. Replace bun:sqlite with the `better-sqlite3` Node package. Replace Bun.Archive with a pure-JS zip/tar library (e.g. `fflate` + `tar`). This makes a prior recommendation to add an 'MCP browser fetch' redundant — the capability is already here.

findings.detail: Full stateful multi-tab browser control with open/close/run actions. Supports headless Puppeteer, spawned Electron apps (CDP attach), and a cmux multiplexer mode. The 'run' action executes JS in the page context with helper APIs (tab.observe, tab.screenshot, tab.fill, tab.click). Includes 14 stealth injection scripts. Discovery mode only (not always-on). 411 lines for the main class; the registry, tab-supervisor, launch, and workers are in the browser/ subdirectory.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS at the tool layer. Dependencies are puppeteer/puppeteer-core (npm) for headless mode and Bun.write for artifact saving. The cmux mode requires oh-my-pi's terminal multiplexer infrastructure. A fork targeting a minimal Node environment needs puppeteer-core and to strip the cmux paths.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — the tool itself is pure-TS. Replace Bun.write with fs.writeFile. Strip cmux references. A prior recommendation to 'add an external MCP for browser' is redundant; this capability is already built-in. The browser subtree (browser/, puppeteer stealth scripts) ports cleanly.

findings.detail: Unified file and URL reader. For local files: reads text, binary, images, SQLite schemas, archives, Jupyter notebooks, and directories (tree view). For URLs: delegates to fetch.ts `executeReadUrl`. Uses pi-natives `summarizeCode` for code summary mode, `glob` for directory listing, bun:sqlite for SQLite, and Bun.file for reads. Integrates deeply with the file-snapshot store for hashline editing support.
findings.depsOrPortability: Mixed: pi-natives (summarizeCode, glob — Rust), bun:sqlite, Bun.file/Bun.write. The core read-text-file, URL dispatch, and formatting logic are pure-TS.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — core functionality (text files, URLs via fetch.ts) ports easily. Substitute: fs.readFile for Bun.file, better-sqlite3 for bun:sqlite, drop summarizeCode or replace with a TS approximation. The hashline snapshot system is Bun-independent; it only needs fs.

findings.detail: File writer with conflict detection, hashline-anchored patching, SQLite row upsert/delete, LSP diagnostics integration, and auto-generated file guard. Uses Bun.write for file output and bun:sqlite for SQLite writes. The conflict/hashline patching logic is pure-TS.
findings.depsOrPortability: Bun.write and bun:sqlite are the only Bun-specific calls. Everything else is Node fs or pure-TS.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — replace Bun.write with fs.writeFile and bun:sqlite with better-sqlite3. Strip the LSP writethrough (or keep the noop path). This is core functionality the fork cannot omit.

findings.detail: Shell command execution with PTY support (via pi-natives), interactive mode, streaming output, inline image protocol (sixels), bash command fixups, and critical-pattern safety guards. Uses pi-natives for PTY spawning (Rust) and for sixel detection.
findings.depsOrPortability: pi-natives PTY (Rust) for interactive mode, Bun APIs for process spawning in non-interactive mode. The pi-mono base agent harness has its own ExecutionEnv.exec() abstraction that a fork would use instead.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — pi-mono's harness already provides shell execution via ExecutionEnv. The oh-my-pi bash.ts is the full-featured implementation behind the pi UX, but it cannot be cleanly ported without the pi-native PTY. For Misul Terminal, implement bash on top of pi-mono's ExecutionEnv.exec() or Node child_process directly.

findings.detail: GitHub CLI wrapper and GitHub REST/GraphQL API client. Supports issues, PRs, repos, comments, review, and code search. Uses bun:sqlite for a response cache (github-cache.ts), Bun.write for temp files and artifacts. The API logic itself is pure-TS fetch calls.
findings.depsOrPortability: bun:sqlite (github-cache.ts), Bun.write, Bun.hash. The core GitHub API logic is pure-TS.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — replace bun:sqlite with better-sqlite3 and Bun.write with fs.writeFile. The tool's ~3300 lines are mostly query formatting and response rendering, all pure-TS. This is a high-value tool to include.

findings.detail: In-session phased task list (init/start/done/rm/drop/append/view). Stores state in session entries (pure in-memory). No native deps, no Bun APIs.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS. Zero native or Bun dependencies.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — trivial port, high value for agent workflows. Copy as-is and strip oh-my-pi-specific session entry references, replacing with whatever session store the fork uses.

findings.detail: Multi-cell REPL supporting Python (via Jupyter/IPython kernel subprocess) and JS (persistent V8 context). Python requires a local Python install with ipykernel. JS eval is implemented in pure-TS using a sandboxed VM. Significant infrastructure: eval/, eval/py/ (kernel management), eval/js/ (sandbox).
findings.depsOrPortability: The JS eval backend uses Node's `vm` module — pure-TS/Node. The Python backend manages an ipykernel subprocess — pure-TS/Node. No Bun or Rust deps in the tool layer itself; some Bun.write usage in output artifacts.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — replace Bun.write with fs.writeFile. Both eval backends are Node-compatible. This is a significant effort (eval/ subtree is large) but is a quality multiplier.

findings.detail: SSH command execution via the ssh/ subsystem (connection manager, executor). Pure-TS wrapping native `ssh` binary via child_process. Enables remote file access and command execution; also used to back find/read/search tools via custom operations when an SSH capability is available.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS + system ssh binary. No Bun or native deps in the tool layer.
findings.recommendation: build-new — the ssh/ infrastructure is tightly coupled to oh-my-pi's capability discovery system. For the minimal Pi fork, implement SSH on top of the `ssh2` npm package if needed, or defer.

findings.detail: BM25-scored search over the catalog of discoverable tools (built-ins + MCP). On match, activates the matched tools into the session. Backed by tool-discovery/tool-index.ts which holds the BM25 index logic (pure-TS). This is oh-my-pi's answer to tool-discovery in large MCP catalogs.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS. The BM25 index is pure-TS in tool-discovery/tool-index.ts. No native or Bun deps.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — the BM25 tool-discovery system is pure-TS and directly solves the 'MCP catalog too large' problem. Copy search-tool-bm25.ts and tool-discovery/ as a unit. This makes any prior recommendation to add an external 'MCP tool discovery' MCP redundant.

findings.detail: Four-tool memory subsystem: retain (store facts), recall (retrieve), reflect (generate new memories from session), edit (update stored memories). Backed by either the 'hindsight' or 'mnemopi' backends. Both backends use the session/storage layer, not native deps.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS. No native or Bun deps in the tool files. The backends (hindsight/, mnemopi/) are pure-TS.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — the memory tooling is pure-TS and provides meaningful quality-per-dollar value through persistent context. Extract retain/recall/reflect/edit plus whichever backend (hindsight is simpler) as a vendored unit.

findings.detail: Git-based session checkpoint and rewind. Creates git commits as named checkpoints; rewind restores working tree to a checkpoint. Pure shell/git operations via bash.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS wrapping git CLI via bash/child_process. No native or Bun deps.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — straightforward port. High value for agentic workflows to recover from bad edits.

findings.detail: Image generation via multiple providers: Gemini Imagen, xAI Grok, OpenAI DALL-E, and Stability AI. Uses Bun.file/Bun.write for saving generated images. Pure-TS HTTP calls otherwise.
findings.depsOrPortability: Bun.file, Bun.write. Otherwise pure-TS API calls.
findings.recommendation: skip — not relevant to a coding-agent harness focused on quality-per-dollar for code tasks.

findings.detail: Text-to-speech output via xAI or local Kokoro TTS model. Writes WAV output to disk.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS HTTP calls + wav encoder. No native deps.
findings.recommendation: skip — out of scope for a coding harness.

findings.detail: IRC chat client tool. Pure-TS network code.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS. No native or Bun deps.
findings.recommendation: skip — out of scope.

findings.detail: Reads an image file and sends it to the model as a vision input. Uses pi-natives for image resizing/format conversion (Rust). Pure-TS otherwise.
findings.depsOrPortability: pi-natives image processing (Rust). Otherwise pure-TS.
findings.recommendation: build-new — if vision input is needed, implement via a pure-JS image library (sharp) or simply pass raw base64. The pi-natives resize logic improves token efficiency but is not essential.

findings.detail: Renders Mermaid diagram syntax to PNG using a headless browser (puppeteer). Uses the browser subsystem.
findings.depsOrPortability: Depends on the browser tool's puppeteer infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: skip — niche utility; out of scope for a minimal coding harness.

findings.detail: Background job manager and sub-agent spawner (task tool). Enables spawning concurrent agent instances for parallelism. Deeply tied to oh-my-pi's internal agent session management and process model.
findings.depsOrPortability: Tightly coupled to oh-my-pi's AgentRegistry, task runner, and session infrastructure. Not portable without significant refactoring.
findings.recommendation: build-new — sub-agent spawning is valuable but needs to be re-implemented on top of pi-mono's AgentHarness. Don't vendor oh-my-pi's job.ts; the harness layer is incompatible.

findings.detail: Replaces LLM-summarized compaction with PNG bitmap frames of pixel-font text that vision models read back. Rasterization is done in the Rust pi-natives crate (`renderSnapcompactPng`). The TS layer serializes conversation history, dispatches to the native renderer, and reattaches frames to the compaction summary. Provider-aware frame shapes and budgets are defined in pure-TS. The research/ directory (Python) documents the empirical benchmarks that selected the shape parameters. Only the snapcompact.ts file is production code.
findings.depsOrPortability: Hard Rust dependency: `renderSnapcompactPng` from pi-natives is the entire rasterization engine. The TS orchestration layer (format selection, provider dispatch, summary construction) is pure-TS and could be extracted, but without the native renderer it produces no frames.
findings.recommendation: skip — the native rasterizer is non-negotiable; without it the package is inert. The compaction strategy itself (vision-based archiving) is high-value but cannot be vendored as pure-TS. If the fork later adds the pi-natives binary as a dep, snapcompact becomes trivially available via npm. For now, use pi-mono's LLM summarization compaction (already in the base harness).

findings.detail: Reads ZIP and TAR archives for listing and member extraction. Uses Bun.Archive (Bun's built-in archive API) for TAR/ZIP. Entry point for archive search path resolution in search.ts and fetch.ts.
findings.depsOrPortability: Bun.Archive — a Bun-specific built-in with no Node equivalent. ZIP reading can be replaced with `fflate` (pure-TS), TAR with `tar` npm package.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — replace Bun.Archive with fflate (ZIP) + tar npm (TAR). The entry-listing and member-extraction logic is straightforward.

findings.detail: Inspects SQLite files: lists tables and row counts. Used by both fetch.ts (URL-fetched .db files) and read.ts (local .db files). Uses bun:sqlite.
findings.depsOrPortability: bun:sqlite only. Otherwise pure-TS.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — replace bun:sqlite with better-sqlite3. Trivial drop-in.

findings.detail: Pauses the agent and asks the user a question, resuming with the user's response. Relies on oh-my-pi's client bridge (TUI message queue). Not portable without a UI layer.
findings.depsOrPortability: Tightly coupled to oh-my-pi's TUI client-bridge infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: build-new — implement a minimal version that reads from stdin or uses the pi-mono harness's message queue.

findings.detail: The pi-mono agent package exports: agent loop, AgentHarness with full session management (JSONL/in-memory repos), compaction (LLM summarization), branch summarization, skills loading, system prompt construction, prompt templates, proxy utilities, and a FileSystem/Shell/ExecutionEnv abstraction. Tools are NOT built-in — the harness accepts a `tools` array from the application. The pi-mono coding-agent package is a pre-built binary; its source is not in this repo.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure-TS/Node. The agent package has zero native deps. The harness runs on any Node-compatible runtime.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — this is the confirmed fork base. It provides the agent loop, session persistence, compaction, skills, and prompt templates. All oh-my-pi tools that get ported must be registered in the harness's tools array.

designRecommendation:
The prior synthesis recommendation to 'fork external MCPs for code-search and browser/fetch' is contradicted by direct inspection of the source. oh-my-pi already ships both capabilities as built-in tools: fetch.ts is a 1938-line multi-backend web reader (including HTML-to-markdown, PDF via markit, RSS parsing, llms.txt discovery) and browser.ts is a full stateful Puppeteer controller. Both are portable to Node with Bun API substitutions only. No external MCP is needed for either.

The portability summary by capability tier:

TIER 1 — port directly as pure-TS (high value, low effort): fetch.ts (web reader), search_tool_bm25.ts + tool-discovery/ (BM25 MCP catalog search), todo.ts (task tracker), checkpoint.ts (git checkpointing), memory-retain/recall/reflect/edit (long-term memory), sqlite-reader.ts (swap bun:sqlite for better-sqlite3), archive-reader.ts (swap Bun.Archive for fflate + tar).

TIER 2 — port with moderate effort (Bun API substitution): browser.ts + browser/ subtree (replace Bun.write with fs.writeFile, strip cmux paths, keep puppeteer-core), write.ts and read.ts (replace Bun.file/Bun.write with fs, bun:sqlite with better-sqlite3), gh.ts (same substitutions), eval.ts + eval/ subtree (replace Bun.write with fs.writeFile), find.ts (replace pi-natives glob with fast-glob via the existing FindOperations interface).

TIER 3 — rebuild on pi-mono primitives, do not port oh-my-pi source: bash.ts (use pi-mono's ExecutionEnv.exec()), job.ts/task (spawn sub-agents via pi-mono AgentHarness), ask.ts (stdin or harness message queue), ssh.ts (use ssh2 npm package if needed).

SKIP entirely (Rust non-portable or out of scope): ast-grep.ts, ast-edit.ts (Rust AST — no TS equivalent), snapcompact (Rust rasterizer non-negotiable), image-gen.ts, tts.ts, irc.ts, render-mermaid.ts.

For code-search specifically: search.ts uses the Rust ripgrep binding as its primary path but also contains a complete pure-TS virtual-resource regex engine. The practical approach is to call the system `rg` binary via child_process (same as trafilatura/lynx in fetch.ts), which avoids the native binding while preserving the tool's interface and pagination logic. The search.ts tool schema, result formatting, and pagination are all pure-TS and reusable verbatim.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: A prior synthesis recommended adding an external MCP for browser/fetch capabilities, implying these were absent from the base.
correctedFacts.correction: oh-my-pi ships both fetch.ts (1938-line URL/HTML/PDF/SQLite/archive reader with native, trafilatura, lynx, Parallel, and Jina backends) and browser.ts (full Puppeteer multi-tab controller with open/run/close actions and stealth injection). Both are built-in tools already in the oh-my-pi coding-agent. Neither requires an external MCP. The fetch capability is even partially redundant with a Jina/Parallel MCP because it already calls those APIs as fallback backends.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: A prior synthesis recommended forking an external MCP for code-search, implying the base lacked this capability.
correctedFacts.correction: oh-my-pi's search.ts implements full ripgrep-backed text search, ast-grep.ts implements AST structural search, and search_tool_bm25.ts implements BM25 tool discovery. All three are built-in. The ripgrep binding is Rust-only, but the practical port is to call the system `rg` binary via child_process, which is already the pattern used for trafilatura and lynx in fetch.ts. No external MCP is required.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: The pi-mono coding-agent package contains built-in tools that can be compared directly against oh-my-pi tools.
correctedFacts.correction: pi-mono's coding-agent package (packages/coding-agent) contains only docs, examples, and build entry points — it ships as a pre-built npm binary with no source tools in the repo. The actual pi-mono tool inventory lives in the agent harness (packages/agent), which provides ZERO built-in agent tools. It only provides the harness loop, session persistence, compaction, skills, and an ExecutionEnv/FileSystem abstraction. All tools must be supplied by the application via the `tools` array in AgentHarnessOptions.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: snapcompact can be vendored as pure-TS to improve compaction quality.
correctedFacts.correction: snapcompact's rasterization is entirely in Rust (`renderSnapcompactPng` from pi-natives, documented at snapcompact.ts:51). The TS layer is only orchestration — it calls the native function and reattaches the resulting PNG frames. Without the pi-natives binary, snapcompact produces nothing. It cannot be vendored as pure-TS. The compaction value is real but requires shipping the pi-natives binary as a dep.

topic: Skills loading/execution and memory toolchain: pi-mono vs oh-my-pi

findings.detail: Skills are Markdown files discovered at startup. Discovery rules: if a directory contains SKILL.md it is treated as a skill root (no further recursion); otherwise direct .md files at the scan root are loaded. Two default scan roots are checked: agentDir/skills (user scope) and cwd/.pi/skills (project scope). Additional explicit skill paths can be provided via CLI. Skills must have a YAML frontmatter block with a non-empty `description` field; `name` falls back to the parent directory name. A `disable-model-invocation: true` frontmatter key hides the skill from the system-prompt listing while still allowing explicit /skill:name invocation. Name collision is resolved first-writer-wins with the lower-priority entry emitting a ResourceDiagnostic of type 'collision'. The loaded Skill[] is stored in DefaultResourceLoader and passed to buildSystemPrompt via agent-session.ts:918. No Bun, no SQLite, no external registry — pure Node fs reads.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS (node:fs, node:path, ignore npm package for .gitignore processing). The ignore package is a small pure-JS dep. easy
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — pi-mono already implements this fully; no porting needed. The base loadSkills implementation is clean and directly maps to our fork's needs.

findings.item: pi-mono skill injection into system prompt (formatSkillsForPrompt)
findings.detail: formatSkillsForPrompt emits an XML <available_skills> block. Each <skill> has <name>, <description>, and <location> (absolute path to SKILL.md). The agent is instructed to call the read tool on that path when the task matches the description. Skills with disableModelInvocation=true are excluded. This is injected into the system prompt by buildSystemPrompt (system-prompt.ts:164) only when the read tool is in the active tool set.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, zero deps. easy
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — already present in pi-mono; adopt unchanged.

findings.detail: When user input starts with /skill:, the session reads the skill file, strips frontmatter, and wraps the body in a <skill name='...' location='...'> XML block that instructs the agent references are relative to skill.baseDir. Arguments after the name are appended verbatim. This is applied at three call sites: when sending user messages (line 1028), during steer (line 1216), and a third path (line 1236). There is NO skill:// URL expansion in bash commands in pi-mono — that feature exists only in oh-my-pi.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS. easy
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — already present. No porting needed.

findings.detail: Extensions are TypeScript files loaded at runtime via jiti (createJiti). In the compiled Bun binary, virtualModules maps bundled packages so extensions can import @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent etc. without filesystem resolution. In Node/dev mode, aliases resolve to node_modules. An extension exports a default factory function (ExtensionFactory) that receives an ExtensionAPI; calling registerTool/registerCommand/registerShortcut/registerFlag on the API wires the extension. Discovery: cwd/.pi/extensions/ (project) then agentDir/extensions/ (global) then explicit CLI paths. Directories are scanned for index.ts/index.js or a package.json with a `pi.extensions` array. Extensions own no state across sessions; the runtime (ExtensionRuntime) is rebuilt per-session and provides action methods (sendMessage, setModel, etc.) that delegate to the live session after bindCore() is called.
findings.depsOrPortability: Bun binary for production (virtualModules path), but the jiti path works under Node. The Bun-specific path (isBunBinary check) is needed only for the distributed binary. The extension API surface itself is pure-TS. moderate — the fork runs under Bun, so this is directly reusable; the isBunBinary branching should be preserved.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — already present and functional. Extensions are the portability mechanism for oh-my-pi tools into our fork.

findings.detail: Central coordinator that owns skills, extensions, prompt templates, themes, AGENTS.md context files, and system prompt overrides. reload() is the main lifecycle method: it calls packageManager.resolve() to get enabled resource paths (pulled from settings + installed packages), then calls loadSkills() with those paths. An extensionsOverride/skillsOverride callback pattern allows programmatic mutation at startup. extendResources() allows post-init injection (used by extensions that register skill paths dynamically). Skills, extensions, and prompt templates are all co-managed here and their source metadata (scope: user/project/temporary, origin: top-level/package) is tracked for UI display.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS plus node:fs. easy
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — this is the integration layer our fork relies on.

findings.item: oh-my-pi skill:// URL expansion in bash commands (expandSkillUrls / expandInternalUrls)
findings.detail: Before executing a bash command, oh-my-pi scans it with SKILL_URL_PATTERN regex for skill:// tokens. Each token is resolved by matchSkillName (longest-prefix match with colon-delimited namespace/suffix handling) against the registered Skill[]. The skill URL can address the SKILL.md file itself (skill://name) or any file within the skill's baseDir (skill://name/relative/path). The resolved filesystem path is shell-escaped with single quotes and substituted back. Also supports a broader INTERNAL_URL_PATTERN_INCLUDING_NORMALIZED_LOCAL that handles agent://, artifact://, memory://, rule://, and local:// schemes through a pluggable InternalUrlResolver. Pi-mono has NO equivalent: its bash tool does not expand skill:// or any internal URL scheme.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS (node:fs/promises, node:path). Zero external deps beyond what pi-mono already has. easy
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — this is an additive pure-TS feature. The Skill[] type is already identical in shape to pi-mono's. Add expandInternalUrls() call in the fork's bash tool (core/tools/bash.ts) after input validation, passing resourceLoader.getSkills().skills. The internal router for non-skill schemes can start as a stub. The portability blocker is identifying WHERE in the bash tool call chain to inject it — the pi-mono bash.ts does not currently expose a pre-execute hook, so the injection requires a small surgical edit to that file.

findings.detail: ManageSkillTool is gated behind autolearn.enabled setting (createIf returns null otherwise). It accepts create/update/delete operations on SKILL.md files confined to ~/.omp/agent/managed-skills/<name>/SKILL.md. writeManagedSkill enforces: sanitizeSkillName (kebab-case, 1-64 chars), sanitizeManagedDescription (strips < > backtick control chars), MAX_MANAGED_SKILL_BYTES=64000 cap, O_CREAT|O_EXCL for create (atomic, no TOCTOU), O_NOFOLLOW for update (rejects symlinks), hardlink count check (prevents writing to user-authored files via hard link). A per-name Promise chain serializes concurrent mutations. deleteManagedSkill similarly guards against symlinked directories. YAML.stringify from Bun is used for frontmatter serialization. Managed skills are loaded dead-last in skill discovery (authored skills always win by name). The autolearn/managed-skills.ts file uses `YAML from 'bun'` directly.
findings.depsOrPortability: The YAML.stringify call on line 76 of managed-skills.ts uses Bun's built-in YAML module. Everything else is node:fs. moderate — the Bun.YAML dependency must be replaced with a pure-TS YAML serializer (e.g., js-yaml or a 3-line manual YAML emitter for a simple 2-key object). The security hardening (O_NOFOLLOW, hardlink check, symlink root guard) is valuable and should be preserved verbatim. The YAML dependency is the only real portability blocker and is trivially replaceable.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — high value. The managed-skills isolation (separate directory, collision detection with authored skills, atomic writes) is the right design and the implementation is solid. Replace YAML.stringify with a minimal inline serializer or js-yaml. Wire it via the pi-mono extension system (register as an inline ExtensionFactory) to avoid touching pi-mono's core.

findings.detail: An orchestrating tool that combines memory retention and optional skill creation in one call. Gated behind autolearn.enabled AND a live memory backend (hindsight, mnemopi, or local). When backend=local it calls localBackend.save() which routes to saveLearnedLesson() in memories/index.ts, appending a neutralized+redacted lesson line to learned.md (newest-first, max 100 entries, 2000-char content cap). When backend=hindsight it calls state.enqueueRetain(). The skill creation branch calls writeManagedSkill identically to ManageSkillTool. The portability of the memory-persist path depends on which backend is in scope.
findings.depsOrPortability: moderate — the skill side is the same as manage_skill (same YAML dep). The memory side's portability depends on backend: local backend paths are pure-TS (Bun.file/Bun.write); hindsight/mnemopi are remote service clients not present in pi-mono.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — port the local-backend path only. The learn tool with memory.backend=local is a tractable pure-TS port: it appends lessons to a flat learned.md file with no service dependency. Defer hindsight/mnemopi backends entirely.

findings.detail: Subscribes to session events. After a turn that ran >= autolearn.minToolCalls tool calls (default 5), and not in plan mode or goal mode, it injects a hidden custom message (customType: 'autolearn-nudge') to prompt the agent to call manage_skill or learn. With autolearn.autoContinue=true it triggers a synthetic continuation turn instead of a passive reminder. Guards against re-triggering from its own continuation. Installed once per top-level session (taskDepth 0). Uses session.sendCustomMessage() which is a pi-agent-core API. No Bun deps.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS. Depends on pi-agent-core's AgentSession.subscribe() and sendCustomMessage() event bus APIs. The pi-mono AgentSession (core/agent-session.ts) has an eventBus but the sendCustomMessage shape needs verification against pi-mono's API surface. moderate
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — but only after manage_skill is wired. The controller is the trigger mechanism; it is low complexity but depends on pi-mono's session event shape being compatible. Check pi-mono's agent-session for equivalent subscribe/sendCustomMessage before porting.

findings.item: oh-my-pi local memory backend (two-phase extract/consolidate pipeline)
findings.detail: Phase 1 (extraction): runs at session startup as a background task (top-level sessions only, requires a persisted session file). Reads .jsonl session files from the session directory, upserts them into a SQLite job queue (bun:sqlite), claims unprocessed threads, and for each calls the LLM with stage_one_system.md + stage_one_input.md template (variables: thread_id, response_items_json). The model returns JSON {raw_memory, rollout_summary, rollout_slug}. Secrets are redacted before storage. Up to stage1Concurrency=8 threads run in parallel. Phase 2 (consolidation): after Phase 1, tries to claim a per-cwd global consolidation job (lease + heartbeat pattern prevents double-run across concurrent processes). Reads all stored stage1 outputs from SQLite, feeds them to a second LLM call (consolidation_system.md + consolidation.md; variables: raw_memories, rollout_summaries). Output JSON {memory_md, memory_summary, skills[]} is applied to disk: MEMORY.md, memory_summary.md, and per-skill SKILL.md files under a project-scoped directory. Injection: buildMemoryToolDeveloperInstructions reads memory_summary.md and learned.md and injects them as a 'Memory Guidance' block in the system prompt append section (capped at summaryInjectionTokenLimit=5000 tokens). Storage module uses bun:sqlite with WAL mode and a busy_timeout. Both LLM calls use pi-ai's completeSimple which works with any configured provider. Phase 1 uses 'default' model role; Phase 2 uses 'smol' role with fallback chain.
findings.depsOrPortability: hard — bun:sqlite is used directly in storage.ts (not via a wrapper). Bun.file(), Bun.write() are used throughout memories/index.ts for file I/O. YAML.stringify in managed-skills.ts. The Bun.Glob used in extensibility/skills.ts. The SQLite schema and job queue are non-trivial (WAL, leases, heartbeats, watermarks, multi-process coordination). Replacing bun:sqlite with better-sqlite3 or sqlite3 is mechanical but requires attention to the busy_timeout pragma and WAL config. Bun.file/Bun.write replacements are trivial (fs.readFile/fs.writeFile). The LLM call path (completeSimple from @oh-my-pi/pi-ai) must map to pi-mono's equivalent SDK call.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS with caution — the algorithmic value (two-phase extract+consolidate, per-project isolation, SQLite job queue preventing double-run, secret redaction, injection budget) is high and directly serves quality-per-dollar. However, the Bun-specific surface is significant: bun:sqlite, Bun.file, Bun.write, Bun.YAML, Bun.Glob. Since the fork IS a Bun-based project (pi-mono compiles to a Bun binary), these are not blockers — they will work. The question is whether to treat the memories/ module as a vendored chunk or rewrite with Node APIs. Recommendation: vendor the memories/ and autolearn/ directories as-is (they will run under Bun), adapt only the import paths (@oh-my-pi/* -> fork equivalents) and the completeSimple call site to use pi-mono's SDK. This is a medium-sized porting job (~1-2 days) with clear boundaries.

findings.item: oh-my-pi memory tools (retain, recall, reflect, memory_edit)
findings.detail: retain (MemoryRetainTool): queues items to the hindsight or mnemopi backend session state. No local-backend path — only hindsight and mnemopi. recall (MemoryRecallTool): calls state.client.recall() (hindsight REST API) or state.recallResultsScoped() (mnemopi). reflect (MemoryReflectTool): calls state.client.reflect() (hindsight) which synthesizes an answer server-side, or state.formatContextScoped() (mnemopi). memory_edit (MemoryEditTool): mnemopi-only, calls state.editScopedMemory(). All four are gated by memory.backend setting. Hindsight is a proprietary external API service; mnemopi is a separate oh-my-pi package with its own SQLite-backed semantic memory. Neither hindsight nor mnemopi exists in pi-mono.
findings.depsOrPortability: avoid — hindsight and mnemopi are oh-my-pi proprietary services. The retain/recall/reflect tools have no local-backend path for structured semantic search. Only the learn tool (which calls localBackend.save) has a viable local path. These four tools are not portable without porting hindsight or mnemopi.
findings.recommendation: skip — these tools require hindsight (external REST API) or mnemopi (oh-my-pi embedded semantic store). Neither is available in our fork. The local-backend alternative (learn tool + memory summary injection) covers the quality-per-dollar case without these dependencies.

findings.item: oh-my-pi skill discovery (capability API + managed-skills integration)
findings.detail: Oh-my-pi wraps skill loading in a capability API (loadCapability from src/discovery). It supports 9 source toggles (codex:user, claude:user, claude:project, native:user, native:project, agents:user, agents:project, custom, managed). Managed skills are loaded dead-last; authored skills of any enabled source win by name. Filtering supports glob patterns (ignoredSkills, includeSkills) via Bun.Glob. The buildSkillPromptMessage function (used for /skill:name) reads the file with Bun.file(). The setActiveSkills/getActiveSkills module-global snapshot is used by the skill:// URL protocol handler to avoid threading Skill[] through every call site. This is a significantly more complex discovery system than pi-mono's. The source-toggle system and capability API are oh-my-pi-specific.
findings.depsOrPortability: hard — Bun.Glob, Bun.file, and the capability API (src/discovery) are oh-my-pi-specific. The discovery/capability layer is an entire subsystem.
findings.recommendation: skip — pi-mono's own loadSkills is sufficient for the fork. The toggle system and capability API are oh-my-pi-internal. The only piece worth extracting is the managed-skills isolation pattern (already covered by the manage_skill port recommendation).

designRecommendation:
The pi-mono base already provides a complete, clean skill subsystem (discovery, injection, /skill:name expansion) that requires zero changes for the fork. The three worthwhile additions from oh-my-pi are: (1) skill:// URL expansion in bash commands (bash-skill-urls.ts) — a pure-TS additive change, inject a call to expandInternalUrls() in the fork's bash tool pre-execute path; (2) the manage_skill tool + autolearn/managed-skills.ts infrastructure — port as pure-TS, replacing only YAML.stringify with js-yaml or a 3-line manual serializer; wire it as an inline ExtensionFactory so it does not touch pi-mono's core; (3) the local-backend two-phase extract/consolidate memory pipeline (memories/index.ts + memories/storage.ts) — vendor as-is since the fork runs under Bun, adapt only import paths and the completeSimple call site.

Do NOT port: retain/recall/reflect/memory_edit tools (require hindsight/mnemopi services), the oh-my-pi capability/discovery subsystem (the pi-mono loadSkills is simpler and sufficient), or the AutoLearnController until manage_skill is confirmed working in the fork's session event model.

Priority order for quality-per-dollar: (1) managed-skills + manage_skill tool — self-improving skill library with no service dependency; (2) local memory pipeline — cross-session knowledge accumulation, the single highest-leverage feature; (3) skill:// URL expansion — convenience, lowest priority.

correctedFacts.correction: Pi-mono has NO skill:// URL expansion in bash commands whatsoever. Grep of pi-mono/src for 'skill://' returns zero matches. The skill:// expansion in bash commands is exclusively an oh-my-pi feature implemented in bash-skill-urls.ts. Pi-mono only has /skill:name slash command expansion (_expandSkillCommand in agent-session.ts) which is a different mechanism that inlines the skill body into the user message.
