topic: Permission / Sandbox / Tool-Authorization Model — oh-my-pi vs pi-mono

findings.detail: Defines three capability tiers (read / write / exec) and three session modes (always-ask / write / yolo). Resolution order per tool call: (1) tool's own approval(args) function returns a ToolApprovalDecision (bare tier string or {tier, reason, override}); (2) user per-tool config override (tools.approval.<name>: allow|deny|prompt); (3) mode-vs-tier rank comparison. In yolo mode user per-tool deny still blocks; override:true on a tool forces a prompt even in write mode. The 'deny' policy throws immediately with a clear remediation message. Exports resolveApproval, requiresApproval, formatApprovalPrompt — all pure TS, no runtime deps beyond @oh-my-pi/pi-agent-core types (lines 9–11). The mode default in settings-schema.ts:2899 is 'yolo', so new sessions are unrestricted unless the user configures otherwise.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure TS. The only imported type, ToolTier / ToolApprovalDecision from @oh-my-pi/pi-agent-core (packages/agent/src/types.ts lines 460–472), is a handful of string union types fully portable to any TS project. No Bun APIs, no Rust. Easy.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — pi-mono's agent package (packages/ai + packages/agent) already defines ToolTier and ToolApproval identically (verified: agent/src/types.ts:460–472). The three-mode/three-tier resolution logic is the exact right model and fits Misul cleanly. Port approval.ts as a pure-TS module (strip the @oh-my-pi/ import alias, point at your agent types). This is ~190 lines with no dependency risk.

findings.item: bash.ts — CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS inline blocklist + per-call override escalation
findings.detail: The BashTool.approval function (lines 354–361) checks the command string against CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS (lines 51–90) before every call. If matched, it returns {tier:'exec', override:true, reason:'Critical pattern detected'}, which forces a user prompt regardless of session mode (even write mode). The 17-pattern list covers: recursive delete to root (rm -rf /), sudo rm, chmod/chown -R to /, fork bombs, disk/filesystem destruction (mkfs, dd to /dev/, shred), /etc/passwd|shadow|sudoers writes, curl|wget|fetch piped to shell or in eval, kill PID 1, and nc -e/-c exfil. Patterns are anchored carefully to avoid false positives (e.g. shutdown is anchored at command position). The bash tool itself has no OS-level sandbox; protection is purely regex-on-command-string applied before execution. bash-command-fixup.ts strips trailing | head/tail and redundant 2>&1 but that logic delegates to a Rust native (@oh-my-pi/pi-natives) for AST-accurate parsing — not usable without the Rust build.
findings.depsOrPortability: CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS: pure TS, trivially portable. The fixup logic (bash-command-fixup.ts) calls applyBashFixups from @oh-my-pi/pi-natives (Rust native module). That specific piece must be skipped or reimplemented in pure TS if Rust is excluded. The rest of bash.ts is deeply coupled to Bun (Bun.write, Bun.sleep, Bun.file, bun:sqlite, etc.) and to many oh-my-pi-specific session/UI types. Reuse the patterns array, not the file. Hard to port wholesale.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — Extract CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS (lines 51–90) and the approval function pattern into Misul's own bash tool. Skip bash-command-fixup.ts entirely (the fixup is a nice-to-have UX polish; correctness does not depend on it and a naive regex strip of '| head' is sufficient). Build Misul's bash tool on top of pi-mono's createBashTool (pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/src/core/tools/bash.ts) which uses node:child_process and has no Bun/Rust deps, then bolt the critical-pattern check onto its beforeToolCall hook.

findings.item: plan-mode-guard.ts — working-tree read-only enforcement during plan mode
findings.detail: When session.getPlanModeState().enabled is true, enforcePlanModeWrite() (lines 121–142) throws ToolError for any write/edit/delete that would touch the working tree. The only allowed writes are to the session-local 'local://' artifact sandbox (verified via targetsLocalSandbox which calls localSandboxRoot then does realpath-normalized containment checks to handle macOS /tmp vs /private/tmp). Move and delete operations are unconditionally rejected in plan mode. resolvePlanPath() handles local://, vault://, and bare filesystem paths (lines 98–113). unwrapHashlineHeaderPath() strips oh-my-pi's hashline display headers ([path#TAG]) before path decisions. Depends on @oh-my-pi/hashline (a checksum/header format library) and resolveLocalRoot/resolveVaultUrlToPath from oh-my-pi's internal-urls module.
findings.depsOrPortability: The core logic (containment check, plan-state check, ToolError throw) is pure TS node:fs/node:path. The hashline unwrapping depends on @oh-my-pi/hashline — a format specific to oh-my-pi's file display system Misul will not have. The local:// URL scheme is also oh-my-pi-specific. Moderate.
findings.recommendation: build-new — Misul does not use hashline or local:// artifact URLs. Build a simpler plan-mode guard from scratch: a boolean session flag plus a path containment check (isUnderCwd). The core idea — 'only allow writes to a designated scratch dir, reject everything else when planning' — is a ~30-line function and does not need the oh-my-pi abstractions. If Misul adds a scratch artifact dir, replicate targetsLocalSandbox without the hashline/URL machinery.

findings.detail: assertEditableFile() (line 293) peeks the first 1024 bytes of a file and checks for known auto-generated markers via regex against leading comment text. Also checks filename patterns (zz_generated.*, *.pb.go, *.gen.ts, etc.). Uses an LRUCache keyed on (mtime, size) to avoid re-reading unchanged files. Gated by settings.get('edit.blockAutoGenerated'). One Bun-specific API: Bun.file(filePath).stat() at line 258, used for mtime/size cache key. The detection logic itself is pure TS. Also imports lru-cache/raw (npm package).
findings.depsOrPortability: Bun.file().stat() is the only Bun API. Replace with fs.stat() from node:fs/promises. lru-cache is a standard npm package. Pure-TS logic otherwise. Easy.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — The detection logic (header comment extraction, marker regex set, filename pattern list) is genuinely valuable quality guardrail and is straightforward to port. Replace Bun.file().stat() with node:fs/promises stat. Replace @oh-my-pi/pi-utils's peekFile with a simple fs.read() of the first 1024 bytes. ~323 lines, entirely standalone after those two substitutions.

findings.detail: checkBashInterception() compiles user-configurable BashInterceptorRule[] into RegExps and matches them against the normalized command. Only blocks if the suggested alternative tool is actually in the active tool set. Default rules (settings-schema.ts:277–295) redirect cat/head/tail/less to 'read' and grep/rg to 'search'. This is UX guidance, not a security control — it nudges the model to use structured tools rather than shelling out. Entirely pure TS, zero deps beyond the settings schema type.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure TS, no external deps, no Bun, no Rust. The BashInterceptorRule interface and DEFAULT_BASH_INTERCEPTOR_RULES constant can be extracted as a ~70-line file. Easy.
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — Worth including. Extract BashInterceptorRule + checkBashInterception + a default ruleset for whatever tools Misul provides. The rules are driven by which structured tools exist (read, search, etc.), so the default list will differ but the engine is reusable verbatim.

findings.detail: A thin TS wrapper (lines 35–37) that delegates entirely to nativeApplyBashFixups from @oh-my-pi/pi-natives, which is a Rust native addon using the brush-parser AST. Strips trailing | head/tail and redundant 2>&1 from single-line commands. The comment at lines 20–21 confirms the heavy lifting is 'in Rust under pi_shell::fixup, driven by the real brush-parser AST.'
findings.depsOrPortability: Hard dependency on @oh-my-pi/pi-natives Rust native module. Avoid.
findings.recommendation: skip — The fixup is a cosmetic UX improvement (model-generated | head is already handled by output truncation). The Rust native requirement makes this a hard exclude. If the behavior matters later, a naive regex strip of trailing '| head [n]' and '2>&1' on single-line input is ~20 lines and handles 95% of cases without AST parsing.

findings.item: pi-mono extension tool_call hook — lightweight pre-execution gate
findings.detail: pi-mono's extension system fires a 'tool_call' event before every tool execution (types.ts lines 857–865, 1020–1024). Handlers receive mutable event.input (can patch args in place) and return {block?: boolean, reason?: string} to veto the call. The permission-gate.ts example (examples/extensions/permission-gate.ts) shows a 34-line extension that regex-matches bash commands and uses ctx.ui.select() to confirm. This is the only permission mechanism in the base pi-mono: no tier system, no approval modes, no per-tool config — just the extension hook. The base bash tool (core/tools/bash.ts) has no built-in blocklist; it uses node:child_process (spawn), is pure Node.js, and carries no approval logic of its own.
findings.depsOrPortability: The extension runner and tool_call event pipeline are pure TS Node.js. No Bun, no Rust. The base pi-mono codebase is the fork target, so this is the starting point.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — The beforeToolCall hook in the agent loop (agent/src/types.ts lines 280–284) is the right integration point in Misul. Wire oh-my-pi's CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS check into that hook. pi-mono's extension 'tool_call' event is also suitable for user-facing permission gates if Misul exposes an extension system.

findings.detail: Tracks per-directory trust decisions in ~/.pi/trust.json using lockfile for concurrency safety. hasTrustRequiringProjectResources() (lines 184–206) checks for .pi/ config dirs and .agents/skills directories under cwd — if any exist, project must be trusted before loading those resources (settings, extensions, prompts). The trust gate is checked at startup (project-trust.ts) before loading project-local extensions, not before individual tool calls. Trust decisions: true/false/null (inherit from parent), with parent-folder and session-only options. This is a project-resource sandboxing concern, not a tool-authorization concern.
findings.depsOrPortability: Pure Node.js TS plus proper-lockfile (npm). No Bun, no Rust. Easy.
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — This is already in the fork base. Misul should adopt it as-is since it gates loading of project-local agent configuration (skills, extensions, prompts) which is exactly the right threat model for a coding agent that operates on arbitrary repos.

findings.item: ExtensionToolWrapper — approval gate wired into the extension execution pipeline
findings.detail: ExtensionToolWrapper.execute() (lines 106–258) is where the three-tier approval system is wired into actual tool execution. Order: (1) resolve approvalMode from context.autoApprove flag or settings; (2) call requiresApproval(); (3) if required and no UI, throw immediately; (4) if UI, show select('Approve'/'Deny'); (5) emit tool_call event to extensions (can block); (6) run tool.execute(); (7) emit tool_result event (can modify). The approval UI prompt is built by formatApprovalPrompt() which calls tool.formatApprovalDetails(args) for per-tool context lines. When the agent runs as a subtask (executor.ts:724–726), approval mode is forced to 'yolo' — the parent task is the authorization boundary.
findings.depsOrPortability: The wrapper is pure TS but tightly coupled to oh-my-pi's ExtensionRunner, UIContext, and Settings types. The logic pattern (check mode, prompt, emit events) is the portable concept.
findings.recommendation: build-new — Implement the same pattern in Misul using the pi-mono beforeToolCall hook (agent/src/types.ts:280–284) which is a clean injection point. That hook receives the tool name and args before execution and can return {block: true, reason: string}. Wire the approval resolution logic there rather than in a wrapper class, keeping it decoupled from any UI framework.

designRecommendation:
Misul should adopt a two-layer permission model that combines what the base pi-mono already provides with the algorithmic value from oh-my-pi.

Layer 1: Project trust (already in pi-mono base). Before loading any project-local resources (.pi/ config, skills, extensions), require explicit user trust for the working directory, stored in ~/.misul/trust.json. Adopt pi-mono's ProjectTrustStore and hasTrustRequiringProjectResources verbatim — they are pure Node.js TS and serve exactly the right purpose. Trust-requiring project resources for Misul would be any .misul/ directory containing settings, extensions, or skills.

Layer 2: Tool-call authorization. Wire a beforeToolCall hook into the agent loop (already defined in packages/agent/src/types.ts). The hook should enforce three things in order:

First, a hard regex blocklist against CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS extracted from oh-my-pi's bash.ts lines 51–90. This runs regardless of session mode and covers the genuinely dangerous shapes: recursive delete to root, mkfs, dd to device, curl-pipe-to-shell, fork bombs, kill PID 1, nc exfil. These 17 patterns are the distilled result of careful calibration (comments in the source explicitly note false-positive avoidance) and should be copied verbatim. When matched, block with a clear error rather than prompting — these are never legitimate in automation.

Second, a three-tier approval mode check, ported from oh-my-pi's approval.ts (~190 lines, pure TS, trivially portable). Map tools to tiers: read-only filesystem and search tools get 'read'; write/edit tools get 'write'; bash and any network-mutating tools get 'exec'. Session mode (always-ask / write / yolo) determines which tiers auto-approve. Default mode for non-interactive (CI) runs should be 'write', not 'yolo', to require confirmation before exec-tier tools. For interactive sessions the user controls the mode.

Third, a per-tool user policy table (tools.approval.<name>: allow|deny|prompt in config), also ported from approval.ts, so users can permanently allow a safe tool or permanently deny a dangerous one without touching the mode.

Do not implement OS-level sandboxing (seccomp, namespaces, containers) in v1. The regex-based critical-pattern check plus user confirmation for exec-tier tools is the right tradeoff for a developer tool that must run arbitrary build commands: it blocks catastrophic accidents while not interfering with legitimate automation. Process-isolation sandboxing belongs in a later phase if Misul runs untrusted agent-generated code at scale.

For plan mode (if Misul implements one): add a simple session boolean flag plus a path containment check in the write/edit tools — do not port the full oh-my-pi plan-mode-guard.ts which is coupled to hashline and local:// URL schemes Misul will not have.

Port the auto-generated file guard (auto-generated-guard.ts) with two substitutions: replace Bun.file().stat() with node:fs/promises stat, and replace peekFile with a direct fs.read(). This is a correctness guard that prevents the model from silently corrupting generated files and belongs in the write and edit tools.

The bash interceptor (redirect cat/grep to structured tools) should be included but is optional in v1. It improves quality-per-dollar by keeping the model in structured tools that return better-formatted context. Extract the engine and define a default ruleset matched to Misul's actual tool inventory.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: The base pi-mono has no permission or approval model.
correctedFacts.correction: pi-mono has two permission mechanisms. First, a project trust system (core/trust-manager.ts + core/project-trust.ts) that gates loading of project-local resources (.pi/ dirs, .agents/skills) using a persistent trust.json store with lockfile safety. Second, the extension system fires a 'tool_call' event before every tool execution (extensions/types.ts lines 857–865) that can block with {block: true}; a working example permission gate is provided at examples/extensions/permission-gate.ts. What pi-mono lacks is the three-tier/three-mode approval engine and the CRITICAL_BASH_PATTERNS blocklist — those are oh-my-pi additions.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: bash-command-fixup.ts is a pure-TS module that can be vendored.
correctedFacts.correction: bash-command-fixup.ts is a one-function wrapper (lines 35–37) that delegates entirely to nativeApplyBashFixups from @oh-my-pi/pi-natives, a Rust native addon. The file's own comment (lines 20–21) states: 'The heavy lifting (tokenization, quoting, heredoc handling, command substitution, nested compound commands) lives in Rust under pi_shell::fixup, driven by the real brush-parser AST.' It cannot be vendored without the Rust build.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: The default approval mode in oh-my-pi is 'always-ask', making it conservative by default.
correctedFacts.correction: The default is 'yolo', verified in settings-schema.ts line 2899: `default: 'yolo'`. In yolo mode all tool tiers auto-approve except tools explicitly denied via per-tool user policy. The conservative 'always-ask' mode must be set explicitly by the user or via the --approval-mode CLI flag.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: auto-generated-guard.ts depends heavily on Bun APIs and cannot be ported.
correctedFacts.correction: auto-generated-guard.ts has exactly one Bun API call: Bun.file(filePath).stat() at line 258, used only to get mtime and size for cache invalidation. The entire detection logic (regex matching, comment parsing, filename patterns, LRUCache) is pure TS. Replacing that single call with node:fs/promises stat() and replacing the peekFile utility with a direct fs.read() produces a fully portable module.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: oh-my-pi subtask agents run under the parent session's approval mode.
correctedFacts.correction: Subtask executor.ts forces approval mode to 'yolo' for child agents (line 724–726), explicitly overriding the parent session's configured mode. The comment states: 'the parent task approval is the authorization boundary.' This means nested agents in oh-my-pi run unrestricted regardless of the outer session's mode.

topic: pi-mono/packages/ai provider routing gaps and corrected provider facts for Misul Terminal fork

findings.item: maxRetries defaults to 0 in anthropic.ts, openai-completions.ts, and openai-responses.ts — no built-in retry at provider level
findings.detail: The requestOptions object in all three providers sets `maxRetries: options?.maxRetries ?? 0`. This delegates to the underlying SDK client (Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK), which then honours its own retry logic only if the caller passes a non-zero value. The default of 0 means the Anthropic and OpenAI SDK clients perform exactly one attempt — no SDK-level 429/5xx retries. The openai-codex-responses.ts provider is the single exception: it implements its own retry loop with `DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES` (line 308), exponential backoff (`BASE_DELAY_MS * 2^attempt`), and a `Retry-After` header cap (`capRetryDelayMs`, controlled by `options?.maxRetryDelayMs`). That loop covers 429s and network errors for the Codex path only.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: build-new — Misul must add its own retry/fallback layer above stream.ts rather than inside providers, since the provider contract is fire-and-return-stream. A wrapper that catches errors from stream.result(), inspects stopReason/errorMessage for 429 or outage patterns, and re-calls stream() with an alternate model is the correct seam. The openai-codex-responses retry logic is a reuse candidate as a pure-TS pattern to port.

findings.item: No per-session cost accumulation anywhere in packages/ai
findings.detail: calculateCost() computes cost for a single AssistantMessage in-place by reading model.cost.{input,output,cacheRead,cacheWrite}. It mutates usage.cost and returns it. There is no session-level accumulator: no running total, no session store, no cost budget enforcement. The Usage type (types.ts:274) carries per-turn cost only. session-resources.ts only manages cleanup callbacks — no financial tracking. The fork must build accumulation from scratch.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: build-new — a SessionCostAccumulator that subscribes to stream events (or wraps complete()) and sums usage.cost.total across turns is trivially portable. The calculateCost function itself is a clean reuse candidate.

findings.item: Provider routing / failover: no cross-provider fallback in base
findings.detail: stream() and complete() are single-shot: they call provider.stream() once and return. There is no fallback list, no circuit breaker, no health tracking. The only multi-provider awareness is OpenRouter's `provider.order` routing hint passed as a request body field (openai-completions.ts:631), which delegates the fallback decision to OpenRouter's servers — invisible to client-side cost or latency accounting. The Model type has no `fallbackChain` field.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: build-new — design a ProviderRouter wrapper around stream/complete that accepts an ordered list of (model, options) pairs and retries the chain on 429/5xx/timeout errors. Classification of retryable vs fatal errors should read errorMessage patterns (already surfaced in AssistantMessage.errorMessage) or the HTTP status via onResponse callback.

findings.item: Cost-aware model selection: no built-in tiering
findings.detail: Model.cost carries per-million-token prices (input, output, cacheRead, cacheWrite). The Model type has no `tier` or `capability` field. Model selection is entirely caller-driven: the caller picks a model object and passes it to stream(). There is no automatic cheap-vs-strong dispatch. The only relevant machinery is thinkingLevelMap (for reasoning effort) and the reasoning: boolean flag. All cost data is present and accurate per-model; the gap is a routing policy layer that uses it.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: build-new — define a TurnClass enum (utility/tool-execution vs reasoning/planning) and a ModelSelector that maps TurnClass to a model object using the cost field as a secondary filter. The existing Model interface is fully sufficient; no schema changes needed.

findings.item: grok reasoning_effort gating via detectCompat isGrok check
findings.detail: detectCompat() sets `isGrok = provider === 'xai' || baseUrl.includes('api.x.ai')` at line 1131, then at line 1141 sets `supportsReasoningEffort: !isGrok && ...`. All xai-provider grok models carry no explicit compat overrides, so the detected value applies. The buildParams paths for OpenAI-style reasoning_effort (lines 620 and 623) both gate on `compat.supportsReasoningEffort`, so reasoning_effort is never emitted for any xai/grok model — including the reasoning: true models (grok-4.20-0309-reasoning, grok-4.3, grok-build-0.1). The grok-4.3 and grok-build-0.1 models have `reasoning: true` and `reasoning: true` respectively but no thinkingLevelMap and no supportsReasoningEffort override, confirming reasoning effort control is fully absent for this provider.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — the gating logic is already correct and intentional. No change needed. When forking, preserve the isGrok exclusion in detectCompat.

findings.item: opencode-go model count and exact IDs
findings.detail: The opencode-go provider block (lines 8568-8803) contains exactly 13 model entries, all using baseUrl https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1 (openai-completions) or https://opencode.ai/zen/go (anthropic-messages). IDs and APIs: deepseek-v4-flash (openai-completions), deepseek-v4-pro (openai-completions), glm-5 (openai-completions), glm-5.1 (openai-completions), kimi-k2.6 (openai-completions), kimi-k2.7-code (openai-completions), mimo-v2.5 (openai-completions), mimo-v2.5-pro (openai-completions), minimax-m2.7 (openai-completions), minimax-m3 (anthropic-messages), qwen3.6-plus (openai-completions), qwen3.7-max (anthropic-messages), qwen3.7-plus (anthropic-messages). Three models use the anthropic-messages API against the /zen/go endpoint.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: reuse-from-base — model metadata is data, copy verbatim. Note that opencode-go is NOT in detectCompat's isNonStandard list (only the generic 'opencode' provider and 'opencode.ai' URL are listed at lines 1122-1123). The opencode-go provider reaches detectCompat as a plain OpenAI-style provider, so supportsReasoningEffort defaults to true — but individual models override this selectively via their compat fields.

findings.item: oh-my-pi auth-retry: structured a/b/c credential rotation with ApiKeyResolver
findings.detail: oh-my-pi adds an ApiKeyResolver function type (vs pi-mono's plain string apiKey). withAuth() runs initial attempt, then on 401 or usage-limit error: step b (refresh same credential, forceRefresh: true), step c (rotate to sibling account). This is pure TS with no Bun or Rust deps. The ProviderHttpError class in errors.ts structures HTTP errors with status, headers, and code fields for downstream classification. pi-mono has none of this — it surfaces errors only as errorMessage strings.
findings.depsOrPortability: pure-TS, easy
findings.recommendation: port-as-TS — withAuth / withOAuthAccess / isAuthRetryableError are self-contained and directly address 401 and usage-limit failover. Port them as the credential layer above Misul's provider router. They complement but do not replace the 429/outage router design since they only handle auth errors.

findings.item: oh-my-pi inband dialect system for text-generation-only models
findings.detail: oh-my-pi implements a dialect layer (anthropic, deepseek, gemini, kimi, qwen3, glm, hermes, etc.) for models that do not support native tool-call APIs. Tools are rendered into the system prompt via InbandTool/renderInbandToolPrompt; tool calls and results are parsed from text via InbandScanner. This enables cheap text-generation models (e.g. opencode-go's minimax, mimo, glm entries) as fallback execution tiers. pi-mono has no equivalent.
findings.depsOrPortability: moderate — depends on @oh-my-pi/pi-catalog/identity (Dialect type) and @oh-my-pi/pi-utils. Dependency graph needs audit before porting.
findings.recommendation: fork-external-MCP — if Misul wants to route utility turns to cheap text-generation models (relevant for quality-per-dollar), the dialect system is the necessary infrastructure. Port the pure-TS scanner/renderer files; replace the @oh-my-pi/pi-catalog import with a local Dialect enum.

designRecommendation:
Design a three-layer routing stack above pi-mono's stream.ts, with no changes to the provider internals.

Layer 1 — ProviderRouter (outage and 429 failover). Accept an ordered list of RouteEntry = { model: Model, options: StreamOptions }[]. Call complete() on the first entry. If AssistantMessage.stopReason is 'error' and errorMessage matches a 429 or transient-outage pattern (HTTP 429, 503, 'overloaded', 'capacity'), advance to the next entry. Preserve the partial context — no replay of already-received content. Each entry can point to a different provider: direct Anthropic, OpenRouter, opencode-go. The onResponse callback in StreamOptions (types.ts:120) gives you the HTTP status before stream body consumption, enabling fast 429 detection without reading the full error body. Circuit-breaker state (consecutive failure count per provider) can be maintained in a Map keyed by model.provider.

Layer 2 — CostAwareSelector (cheap vs strong dispatch). Define TurnClass: 'utility' | 'reasoning'. Utility covers tool execution turns (file reads, shell commands — high volume, low latency requirement, structured output). Reasoning covers planning, summarization, multi-step code generation. Map each TurnClass to a ModelTier = { primary: RouteEntry[], floor: RouteEntry[] } where primary is the preferred model(s) and floor is the budget cap. Use Model.cost.output as the primary sort key for tier selection. For utility turns, target output cost under $2/M (e.g. opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash at $0.28/M output, opencode-go/mimo-v2.5 at $0.28/M). For reasoning turns, allow up to direct Anthropic claude-opus or haiku depending on budget. The SessionCostAccumulator (Layer 3) feeds back into tier selection: if accumulated cost exceeds a per-session budget, the selector shifts all turns to the utility tier.

Layer 3 — SessionCostAccumulator. Subscribe to AssistantMessage.usage.cost.total after each complete() call. Maintain a running session total. Expose currentCost(), remainingBudget(budget), and a budgetExceeded(budget) predicate that the CostAwareSelector polls. No async coordination needed — the accumulator is synchronous state local to the session.

Interaction with grok reasoning: because grok xai models never receive reasoning_effort (supportsReasoningEffort is false in detectCompat), do not route reasoning turns to grok variants unless you are comfortable with uncontrolled reasoning token spend. If used, set model.thinkingLevelMap appropriately or exclude them from the reasoning tier.

Interaction with opencode-go: three models use anthropic-messages API (minimax-m3, qwen3.7-max, qwen3.7-plus) and ten use openai-completions. For the completions models, detectCompat treats opencode-go as standard OpenAI-style (supportsReasoningEffort: true by default), but individual models override via compat fields. deepseek-v4-flash and deepseek-v4-pro set requiresReasoningContentOnAssistantMessages: true, so multi-turn replay must include reasoning_content. Handle this at the ProviderRouter level when replaying history across provider switches by normalizing assistant messages before re-submission.

Auth failover: port oh-my-pi's withAuth/ApiKeyResolver pattern as a credential factory that the ProviderRouter calls when constructing each RouteEntry's apiKey. This handles 401 rotation independently of 429 provider switching. The two concerns are orthogonal and should not be conflated in a single retry loop."

correctedFacts.priorClaim: supportsReasoningEffort = !isGrok gates reasoning_effort for grok-4.x models; reasoning_effort IS sent to grok when model.reasoning is true
correctedFacts.correction: reasoning_effort is NEVER sent to any xai/grok model. detectCompat() at openai-completions.ts:1131 sets isGrok = provider === 'xai' || baseUrl.includes('api.x.ai'), and at line 1141 supportsReasoningEffort = !isGrok (among other exclusions). Both buildParams branches that emit reasoning_effort (lines 620 and 623) are gated on compat.supportsReasoningEffort. No xai model has an explicit compat.supportsReasoningEffort override. The grok reasoning models (grok-4.20-0309-reasoning, grok-4.3, grok-build-0.1) carry reasoning: true but receive no effort parameter — the reasoning: true flag only enables thinking-content parsing in the stream handler.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: opencode-go has a small model count (implied fewer than 10) or a different set of model names than what was cited
correctedFacts.correction: opencode-go has exactly 13 models in models.generated.ts (lines 8568-8803): deepseek-v4-flash, deepseek-v4-pro, glm-5, glm-5.1, kimi-k2.6, kimi-k2.7-code, mimo-v2.5, mimo-v2.5-pro, minimax-m2.7, minimax-m3, qwen3.6-plus, qwen3.7-max, qwen3.7-plus. Three (minimax-m3, qwen3.7-max, qwen3.7-plus) use the anthropic-messages API against https://opencode.ai/zen/go; the remaining ten use openai-completions against https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: pi-mono/packages/ai has no retry logic at all
correctedFacts.correction: The openai-codex-responses.ts provider implements its own retry loop with configurable maxRetries (DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES when not set by caller), exponential backoff, and Retry-After header parsing for 429s. The anthropic.ts, openai-completions.ts, and openai-responses.ts providers default maxRetries to 0 (delegating to SDK defaults, which with 0 means one attempt). So the claim is accurate for all Anthropic-API providers but false for the Codex provider.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: opencode-go provider is treated as non-standard in detectCompat and supportsReasoningEffort is false for it
correctedFacts.correction: detectCompat() lists provider === 'opencode' and baseUrl.includes('opencode.ai') in isNonStandard (lines 1122-1123), but 'opencode-go' is not in that list. The opencode-go models all use baseUrl 'https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1' which DOES include 'opencode.ai', so they do fall into isNonStandard via the URL check. However, supportsReasoningEffort is NOT excluded by isNonStandard — it is only excluded by the specific isGrok, isZai, isMoonshot, isTogether, isCloudflareAiGateway, and isNvidia checks. opencode-go is none of those, so the detected supportsReasoningEffort would be true. Individual opencode-go models then selectively override via their compat fields (e.g. kimi-k2.6 sets supportsReasoningEffort: false explicitly).

topic: Trust Ledger: Correction of laundered/single-source claims from prior capabilities-research synthesis, verified against local pi-mono clone and web sources
designRecommendation:
1. COMPACTION LOGIC: Port pi-mono's flat `shouldCompact` formula verbatim — it is well-tested, self-contained pure TS with no external deps, and ships in the exact fork target. The oh-my-pi `max(15% of contextWindow, reserveTokens)` variant is a more sophisticated threshold that is worth vendoring as a configurable upgrade, but must be treated as additive oh-my-pi logic, not as something already present in the base. Do not conflate the two in specs or PRDs.

2. MCP BENCHMARKS: All numeric MCP claims (939x, 70-95%, 99%, 9-16x) must be treated as directional-unverified marketing figures until a primary reproducible source is found. Do not gate architectural decisions on these numbers. Treat MCP as beneficial for tool-surface expansion and external integrations, with the conservative assumption that token overhead is real and variable depending on server quality, and speed gains are tool-dependent. Budget for MCP latency in the architecture rather than assuming speed gains.

3. ANTHROPIC AUTH/ToS: The Misul Terminal plan must be built exclusively around API key authentication against claude.com/platform. Do not build any feature that relies on OAuth subscription tokens from Pro/Max accounts — the policy has been enforced at least once (April 2026), reversed, then re-proposed (May 2026 credit split), then paused again (June 15 confirmed not going ahead). The instability itself is the risk signal: Anthropic has demonstrated willingness to terminate third-party subscription auth with short notice. The safe path for production agent workloads is the pay-per-use API. Budget all cost projections on API rates, not subscription access.

4. PRICING: Use $5/$25 per MTok (input/output) for claude-opus-4-8 as the current baseline from claude.com/platform/api (fetched June 2026). These figures are confirmed by the official Anthropic platform page and consistent across multiple secondary sources. Apply the standard caveats that Anthropic has changed pricing structures multiple times in 2026 and any cost model should build in a ±20% buffer and be refreshed quarterly.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: pi-mono uses a '15% of contextWindow / max()' compaction threshold formula
correctedFacts.correction: False. The pi-mono compaction threshold is a flat `contextTokens > contextWindow - reserveTokens` check with no percentage component. The `shouldCompact` function at line 196-199 of packages/agent/src/harness/compaction/compaction.ts reads exactly: `return contextTokens > contextWindow - settings.reserveTokens`. The default `reserveTokens` is 16384 (line 114 of the same file, in `DEFAULT_COMPACTION_SETTINGS`). There is no `max()` call, no 15% floor, and no `thresholdPercent` field anywhere in pi-mono. The '15% of contextWindow / max(reserveTokens, 15% of contextWindow)' formula exists only in oh-my-pi, documented at line 413 of oh-my-pi/docs/compaction.md: 'when no positive override is set, the threshold is contextWindow - max(15% of contextWindow, reserveTokens)'. The prior claim conflated oh-my-pi's documentation with pi-mono's code.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: MCP delivers 939x improvement in app-control tasks, 70-95% token reduction, 99% retrieval savings, and 9-16x browser speed gains
correctedFacts.correction: No primary or authoritative source for any of these specific multipliers was found. The oh-my-pi local clone contains no mention of these figures in any documentation file. Web search found no official Anthropic or modelcontextprotocol.io publication with these exact numbers. The closest verified claim is that schema-compression approaches can reduce tool-definition tokens by 70-97% (a GitHub issue on modelcontextprotocol, single-source, not peer-reviewed). All four figures — 939x, 70-95%, 99%, 9-16x — must be treated as directional-unverified: the underlying phenomena (MCP reduces manual glue code, retrieval MCPs can cut lookup token spend, browser MCPs change interaction patterns) are plausible, but no reproducible benchmark with a defined baseline and methodology was located. Label these figures as vendor-directional until a primary reproducible source is identified.
correctedFacts.source: Local clone search: C:\Users\deyan\Projects\Misul Terminal\research\sources\oh-my-pi (no matches found). Web: https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/1576 (schema token reduction, single issue, not peer-reviewed benchmark)

correctedFacts.priorClaim: Anthropic's OAuth/subscription enforcement timeline and ToS risk are stable and predictable
correctedFacts.correction: False. The enforcement history in 2026 shows three distinct policy reversals in under six months, making this an actively unstable area. Timeline: (1) February 19 2026 — Anthropic formally updated ToS to prohibit OAuth tokens from Free/Pro/Max accounts in any third-party tool, restricting them to Claude Code and Claude.ai only. (2) April 4 2026 — Enforcement began; third-party tools blocked. (3) May 2026 — Anthropic reversed the April enforcement and announced a 'credit pool' split model effective June 15. (4) June 15 2026 — Anthropic paused the credit pool change; Agent SDK usage continues drawing from existing subscription pools 'exactly as before'. Current binding rule: OAuth subscription tokens are still formally prohibited by ToS from non-Anthropic tools, but enforcement posture has oscillated. The safe conclusion is: do not build any system dependency on subscription OAuth access; use API key authentication only. This is time-sensitive and cannot be verified from the local clone.

correctedFacts.priorClaim: Claude Opus 4.8 pricing figures are reliable and stable
correctedFacts.correction: Partially confirmed but date-sensitive. The official claude.com/platform/api page (fetched June 17 2026) shows Opus 4.8 at $5/MTok input and $25/MTok output. Fast Mode (2.5x speed) is 2x standard price ($10/$25 per MTok). These figures are corroborated by multiple secondary sources citing the May 28 2026 Opus 4.8 launch. However, Anthropic changed its billing structure at least twice in 2026 (the April enforcement and the paused June 15 credit pool split), and the pricing page itself notes 'Price and plans are subject to change at Anthropic's discretion.' Cost models using these figures should be treated as point-in-time (June 2026) and budgeted with a buffer for revision. The prior claim of a specific Opus 4.8 price should be labeled 'single-source/date-sensitive: verified June 2026, refresh quarterly'.
correctedFacts.source: https://claude.com/platform/api (fetched June 2026, primary source) and https://www.metacto.com/blogs/anthropic-api-pricing-a-full-breakdown-of-costs-and-integration (secondary corroboration)
